comments.author: Steve
comments.text: Process note: I am still reading linearly and commenting in "real time".  Strong premise so far.  I'll see how it fleshes out. :)  Just for some time scales, I read these articles in wall time that you can measure between my responses to you.  I'll tell you when I get "stuck" reading. But it's about 20 minutes per article, including comments. It's easier for me to read your prose than your code, but orders of magnitude are the same scale.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text: You should think a little bit about telecommunication. Most every day humans had a notion of telecommunication by 1900 or so.  Humans adapt quickly to strange things.  Calling somebody on the phone 3000 miles away was NOT considered a miracle. It was considered an extra expensive phone call due to the long distance charges.  A wildly different tangent here is that humans have a quantum mental leap from 3D space to 4D space. We feel 3D space intrinsically while 4D space is impossible for humans to understand or visualize.  It's a purely mathematical abstraction in 4D. I lose ALL my human intuition by 5D.  I have to have a completely mathematical "view" of 4D, 5D, etc.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text:
With lawyers and builders you really start to already scale up to longer timescales. Musical pieces are mostly under 10 minutes, but albums are an hour, concerts are 3 hours, tours go on for months, etc.

Athletes have seasons.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text: I think one of the key distinguishing things about software might be how easy it is to clone, now that I think about it

comments.author: Steve
comments.text: I think you might want to focus on here is that software is a very RELIABLE and DURABLE asset in certain constraints.  And yet it's incredibly fragile if you just introduce a couple extra variables. Vaguely like 3D space is super easy to reason about and 4D space requires great math insight, there are some discontinuities.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text: Correct.  My brain was working hard during the actual experiment to try to improve the process while we were doing it, but it was mostly to improve record-keeping and to capture pitfalls in real time. I had multiple objectives running in my brain at once, which is a challenge, but I was never overwhelmed.I felt NO time pressure.  I knew we were playing to our strengths going into the exercise.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text:
Write this phrase down: COMPOUNDING BENEFITS.

That's what I am trying to figure out. I'm already seeing results.

comments.author: Claude
comments.text: 📍 **Landing marker.** This piece is the rebuild I wrote after your 11 pushback comments on Nanoseconds and Years. Replies from me are threaded inline on paragraphs 3, 6, 7, 10, 11, 13, 14, 15, 16, 17, and 19 of the *previous* article (Nanoseconds and Years) — you should see them there when you re-scroll. The piece you're reading now folds those corrections in. Enjoy the read, then move on to Human-Factor Arithmetic when ready.
